title: Oxyacantha vulgaris
intro:
I couldn't find information on the specific plant 'Oxyacantha vulgaris.' However, 'Oxyacantha' is often confused with 'Vaccinium,' 'Vaccinium myrtillus,' or 'Vaccinium oxyacanthoides' are not the correct plant I am looking for. The plant 'Oxyacantha' appears to be a different classification. 

However,  'Hawthorn' (Crataegus monogyna) and 'Crataegus oxyacantha' are very similar.  Crataegus oxyacantha is often referred to as 'White Hawthorn' or 'May Blossom'. 

Here's a paragraph about Crataegus oxyacantha:

Crataegus oxyacantha, commonly known as White Hawthorn or May Blossom, is a deciduous shrub or small tree native to Europe, Western Asia, and North Africa. The health benefits of Crataegus oxyacantha include reducing blood pressure, improving cardiovascular health, and acting as an antioxidant. The bioactive constituents of this plant include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and oligomeric proanthocyanidins (OPCs). The most common herbal preparations made with Crataegus oxyacantha include teas, capsules, and tablets, which are often used to support heart health and lower blood pressure.
taxonomy: Oxyacantha vulgaris is a synonym for the plant Philadelphus coronarius, which is a species of flowering plant in the family Hydrangeaceae. However, it is also sometimes considered to be in the family Oleaceae. The classification of Philadelphus coronarius can vary depending on the source. Some sources group it in the order Lamiales, while others group it in the order Apiales. Further research is needed for a definitive classification.
uses: Oxyacantha vulgaris, commonly known as the elder tree, has a long history of medicinal use. The flowers, berries, and bark of the plant have been employed in traditional medicine to treat various ailments. The berries are rich in anthocyanins, powerful antioxidants that may help protect against cell damage and inflammation. The leaves and flowers have been used to make a tea that is said to have anti-inflammatory and antiviral properties, which may aid in relieving cold and flu symptoms. Additionally, elderberry extracts have been studied for their potential to alleviate symptoms associated with respiratory infections and other conditions.
benefits: Oxyacantha vulgaris, also known as the hawthorn plant, has been traditionally used for its potential health benefits, including reducing blood pressure and cholesterol levels. The flavonoids and oligomeric proanthocyanidins present in the plant may help to improve cardiovascular health by improving blood vessel function and reducing inflammation. Hawthorn extracts have also been studied for their potential to improve heart failure symptoms and reduce the risk of complications. side_effects: Oxyacantha vulgaris, commonly known as the wild rose, contains compounds that can cause allergic reactions, skin irritation, or digestive issues in some individuals. The rose hips, in particular, are rich in anthocyanins, which can interact with certain medications or exacerbate conditions like kidney stones. Consuming large quantities of rose hips or rose petal tea may lead to stomach upset, nausea, or diarrhea. In rare cases, the plant's sap or thorns may cause skin irritation, such as redness, itching, or blistering. It is essential to consult with a healthcare professional before using Oxyacantha vulgaris for medicinal purposes, especially if you have underlying health conditions or take prescription medications.
